Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon, but it wasn't planned out. I spent about 29 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that time I was just going through the motions, not really absorbing much. I learn better by listening, so I'd sometimes just play lecture recordings while doing other things, which probably wasn't the most effective approach.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd cram everything in right before deadlines, other weeks I'd barely touch anything. It really depended on my mood and whether I had the energy to deal with the coursework. When I was feeling particularly anxious, I'd avoid studying altogether, then have to catch up later in a panic.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification that something was due soon. I wasn't the type to log in just to browse or stay on top of things. Sometimes a classmate would message me about an assignment and that would remind me to check the LMS. It was reactive, not proactive, if I'm being honest with myself.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd log in, do what I needed to do, and log out. There were stretches where I wouldn't log in for days. The peer support intervention helped a bit because other students would remind me about things, but even then it didn't become routine.
Q5: The recorded lectures were the most useful because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times if something didn't click the first time. The videos helped me understand concepts better than reading dense text. I watched quite a lot of video content when I actually engaged with the course.
Q6: I ignored most of the discussion forums and reading materials. They felt tedious and I couldn't focus on them for long. I'd skim readings at best, and I rarely participated in discussions beyond the bare minimum. The written stuff just didn't hold my attention the way audio content did.
Q7: If I could see an immediate connection to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. Otherwise, I'd get bored quickly and my mind would wander. Activities that were interactive or involved listening kept me engaged longer than passive reading. If something felt like busywork, I'd just tune out.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. I had enough trouble keeping up with the mandatory stuff. Required activities at least had the threat of a grade attached, which was sometimes the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of audio files and lecture recordings to my phone. I'd listen to them while commuting or doing chores. My resources are limited so I couldn't always have reliable internet access, so downloading things to use offline was necessary. I also borrowed notes from classmates sometimes.
Q10: There were many times I was listening to downloaded lectures while walking or cooking, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also have long conversations with peers about course content that happened over text or in person. The LMS only saw me when I was actively clicking around, not when I was actually thinking about the material.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it three or four times over the following week. The LMS would only show one access, but I was engaging with that content repeatedly. Same with PDFs that I'd print out at the library and annotate by hand.
Q12: There were several weeks where my anxiety got really bad and I just couldn't bring myself to engage. I'd feel overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind, which made me avoid it even more. The boredom was constant, but the anxiety spikes made everything worse. I'd just shut down and ignore everything.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other life stuff, the LMS was the first thing I neglected. Having limited resources meant I was sometimes dealing with practical problems that took priority over coursework. Fatigue was a big one—I'd open the LMS intending to work but just stare at the screen blankly.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, click through some materials, maybe start a video, but my mind would be completely elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually learning anything. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: All the time. I'd put off assignments even when I knew they were important because the thought of starting them made me anxious. The longer I waited, the more anxious I got, which made me avoid them even more. It was a vicious cycle that I never really figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS seriously. I'd scramble in the last day or two before something was due. My activity probably spiked dramatically right before deadlines and dropped to almost nothing afterward. It wasn't healthy but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got feedback, I'd already mentally moved on to the next crisis. Sometimes good feedback made me feel briefly motivated, but that feeling never lasted. I didn't really know how to use feedback constructively.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming to me. There was always something due or something to keep track of. I would have done better with fewer, clearer milestones. Having eight online courses at once meant I was constantly juggling and dropping balls.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it suited how I learn. But the amount of reading and the discussion requirements made it harder. I also found the interface confusing sometimes, which added to my frustration. Clearer organization would have helped me a lot.
Q20: That makes sense to me. You can click around a lot without actually producing anything meaningful. Maybe they're anxious like me and spend time in the LMS without being able to focus enough to complete work. Or they're exploring, trying to figure out what they need to do, but getting stuck.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. My attendance and LMS activity were pretty low, but I still managed a Merit grade. I did a lot of learning offline through audio recordings and peer discussions. The exam score doesn't always reflect how often you logged in—it reflects whether you understood the material somehow.
Q22: The log can show when you clicked on things and how long you spent on pages, but it can't show what was going on in your head. It misses offline studying, conversations with classmates, and whether you were actually focused or just had the tab open. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and being mentally present. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, real engagement happened rarely—usually when a topic genuinely interested me or when I was listening to a good lecture. Most of the time I was just surviving.
Q24: The anxiety I dealt with constantly wouldn't show up in any data. Neither would the boredom that made everything feel like a chore. The peer support I received helped me emotionally even when it didn't translate to more LMS activity. My real struggles and my real learning happened in places the system couldn't see.
